claim: The condition is inverted: the comment says urllib3 >= 1.21.1 must be enforced, but `if not (major == 1)` asserts minor >= 21 only when major != 1, so urllib3 1.20 wrongly passes while urllib3 2.x (e.g. 2.0.7) wrongly fails the compatibility check.

The validation in is_prepared is placed under `if TYPE_CHECKING:`, which is always False at runtime, so the function unconditionally returns True (line 52) and never actually checks that url and method are non-None — the type guard falsely narrows unprepared requests, enabling downstream None dereferences.

The MaxRetryError handler ends with `pass` instead of raising ConnectionError, so unhandled retry-exhaustion errors (e.g. plain connection refusal) are silently swallowed and execution falls through to `build_response(request, resp)` where `resp` is unbound, causing an UnboundLocalError instead of a proper ConnectionError.

Inverted condition: `if not (isinstance(e, _SSLError))` raises SSLError for non-SSL errors (e.g. a ReadTimeoutError would be wrongly reported as SSLError before the elif is reached), while actual _SSLError instances skip the SSLError branch, fall through the ReadTimeout/InvalidHeader elifs, and are re-raised as the raw urllib3 exception.

The condition `if not (TYPE_CHECKING):` is inverted — the type-only imports (`Unpack`, `_types`) should be guarded by `if TYPE_CHECKING:`, so as written they execute at runtime (risking an ImportError on `typing_extensions`) while being invisible to type checkers, which is the opposite of the intended behavior.

s_auth is obtained via r.headers.get("www-authenticate") with no default (line 291), so on a 4xx response lacking the WWW-Authenticate header s_auth is None and s_auth.lower() raises AttributeError instead of returning the response.

The TYPE_CHECKING condition is inverted — `if not (TYPE_CHECKING)` executes `import chardet` unconditionally at runtime, raising ImportError when chardet is not installed, while the graceful `_resolve_char_detection()` fallback (which also tries charset_normalizer) only runs during static type checking.
get_header() ignores its `default` parameter and always calls headers.get(name) without it, so the supplied default is never returned for missing headers.
getheaders() is missing a `return` — it computes self._headers.getheaders(name) but discards the result, so the method always returns None.
Uses `+` between two sets (TypeError: unsupported operand) instead of set difference `set(kwargs) - set(result)`, so create_cookie() always crashes and can never validate kwargs.
The ValueError from an invalid non-integer max-age is silently swallowed with `pass`, leaving expires=None so a bad max-age silently produces a session cookie instead of raising an error.

The condition is inverted with `not (...)`: the original guard should assign `self.request = response.request` only when response is not None, request is unset, and response has a request attribute, but as written the body executes in the opposite cases — including when `response` is None, causing an AttributeError on `response.request` (line 34) for the common case of raising RequestException wit

The condition `if not (OpenSSL)` is inverted — it enters the block precisely when OpenSSL is None (import failed), causing an AttributeError on `OpenSSL.__version__` at line 95, and skips populating pyopenssl_info when OpenSSL is actually available.
iter_lines uses subtraction `(pending) - (chunk)` instead of concatenation `pending + chunk`, which raises TypeError at runtime whenever a pending partial line must be joined with the next chunk.

Inverted condition `if not (self.encoding is None)` replaces an explicitly-set encoding with apparent_encoding and skips auto-detection when encoding is None (the opposite of the intended fallback), so a user-set encoding is ignored and unset encodings never get detected.
The condition is inverted with `not` — it aliases every module EXCEPT the chardet-compatible package under `requests.packages.*`, while skipping the chardet modules it was meant to alias.

The auth-strip condition is inverted with `not (...)`, so the Authorization header is deleted precisely when it should be kept (and raises KeyError when absent) while credentials are leaked on cross-host redirects where stripping is required.
Elapsed time is computed as `preferred_clock() + start` instead of `preferred_clock() - start`, producing a wildly wrong r.elapsed value.
The docstring is combined with the `-` operator instead of `+` (`(__doc__ + "\n") - ("\n".join(...))`), which raises TypeError at import time since strings do not support subtraction.

The None-check is inverted (`if not (data is None)`), so any provided data is discarded and replaced with an empty dict, while a None value is passed through to `self.update(None)`, which raises TypeError.
The `get` implementation ignores its `default` parameter, calling `self.__dict__.get(key)` without passing `default`, so a caller-supplied default is never returned.

In atomic_open, the except block uses `pass` instead of re-raising, so any exception during the write is silently swallowed and the context manager exits successfully even though the target file was never written (upstream code has `raise` here).

In _parse_content_type_header, the parameter value is sliced as `param[(idx) - (1):]` instead of `param[idx + 1:]`, so the value wrongly includes the character before '=' and the '=' itself (e.g. 'charset=utf-8' yields 't=utf-8'), breaking charset extraction in get_encoding_from_headers.
